                 Ray Marshall, Secretary

                BUREAU OF LABOR STATISTICS
                   Julius Shiskin, Commissioner

             Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
         Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
         Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
         ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
         Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
         of the Census (Department of Commerce), State
         Employment Security Agencies, and State Depart-
         ments of Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of
          Labor Statistics. A brief description of the co-
         operative statistical programs of the BLS with
         these agencies is presented in the Explanatory
         Notes. The State agencies are listed on the inside
         back cover.

             Employment and Earnings may be ordered
         through the Superintendent of Documents, U.S.
         Government Printing Office, Washington, D.C.
         20402. Subscription price per year $18 domes-

                                    Nonagricultural Employment as Measured
                                                  by Two Series
                                                                                                                                "Gloria P. Green


                 Each month the Bureau )f Labor Statistics analyzes and        enterprises, and persons "with a job but not at work" during
             publishes estimates of employment in nonagricultural in-          the survey week who were not paid for the time absent
             dustries based on data collected from two independently           (e.g., strikers).
             derived sample surveys, the Current Population Survey                The establishment survey estimates the number of paid
             (household data) and the Current Employment Survey                wage and salary employees (regardless of age) on nonagri-
             (establishment data). Both series have long histories, and        cultural payroll records. Employees who worked at more
             both are important in gauging the performance of the              than one job during the survey week or who otherwise
             economy. Their respective trends have been generally com-         appear on more than one payroll are counted separately
             parable over time, but the levels of the series differ. Indeed,   for each appearance. Such persons are counted only once
             at times, significant differences occur with respect to the       in the household survey and are classified in the job at
             magnitude and direction of month-to-month and annual              which they worked the greatest number of hours.
             changes, the timing and extent of movements during
             business cycles, and other shortrun trends.                       Measured differences
                 The differences occur for several reasons. Data from the
             Current Population Survey (CPS) are obtained from a                   Table 1 shows annual average levels for 1975-77 of the
             sample of households and measure the work status of               two series, including adjustments for basic quantifiable
             individuals, whereas Current Employment Survey (CES)              differences. The reconciliation technique involves sub-
             data are derived from payroll records of firms and thus are       tracting estimates of the following groups from the house-
             essentially a count of occupied jobs. Although conceptual         hold series on nonagricultural employment: Self-employed
             differences between the series can be reconciled or explained     workers, unpaid family workers, private household workers,
             in part and a number of the differences in scope can be           and workers on unpaid absences from their jobs during the
             adjusted, the discrepancies which result from differences in      survey week. Then estimates of employed 14- and 15-year-
             sampling techniques, collection, and estimation methodo-          olds in nonagricultural wage and salary jobs, (excluding
             logy cannot be readily quantified in terms of their effect        private household work) and workers employed in agri-
             on the levels or movements of the two series.                     cultural services 2 (both series are also collected in the
                                                                               CPS) are added to the household series.
                 Bureau publications that present data from the two
             surveys usually provide some explanation of the con-

                                                                    Explanatory Notes

                                These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                (A tables), Establishment Data (B, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data {E table) pub-
                                lished in Employment and Earnings.



                                                                            Introduction

                 The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two major            Employment
              sources: (1) HousehoJd interviews, and (2) reports from employers.
